name: Microsoft Entra ID (Azure AD) API
what_it_does: Microsoft Entra ID (formerly Azure Active Directory) provides identity and access management APIs for managing users, groups, service principals, app registrations, conditional access policies, and OAuth 2.0/OIDC authentication flows.
use_cases: Provisioning and deprovisioning user accounts and group memberships automatically in response to HR system events or onboarding workflows
Creating and managing service principals and app registrations for agents that need to authenticate as applications to Azure resources
Querying user attributes, manager hierarchies, and group memberships to make authorization decisions in enterprise agents
Implementing conditional access policy evaluation and sign-in risk checks to enforce Zero Trust access decisions
Automating role assignment (RBAC) to Azure resources by assigning or revoking directory roles and Azure resource roles for users and managed identities
not_for: Non-Azure identity providers — if your org uses Okta, Auth0, or Ping Identity as the primary IdP, those native APIs are a better fit
Simple end-user authentication in consumer apps — Azure AD B2C is a separate product; Entra ID targets workforce identity
Directory lookups in environments without Microsoft 365 or Azure — Entra ID requires an Azure tenant
best_when: Your organization runs on Microsoft 365/Azure and you need to automate enterprise identity tasks — user lifecycle, group management, app permissions, or audit log retrieval — within an existing Entra tenant.
avoid_when: You only need simple OAuth 2.0 token validation and have no Microsoft tenant — adding Entra ID solely for auth introduces unnecessary Azure dependency.

auth.notes: OAuth 2.0 and OIDC are the primary auth mechanisms. Application permissions (client credentials flow) are used for agent/daemon scenarios — no user interaction required. Delegated permissions require a signed-in user context. Managed identity is the preferred method for Azure-hosted agents. Certificate-based authentication is supported and preferred over client secrets for application auth. Scopes follow the pattern https://graph.microsoft.com/{scope}.

pricing.free_tier_limits: Entra ID Free tier included with any Azure or Microsoft 365 subscription. Covers basic user/group management, SSO for 10 apps, and MFA. Microsoft Graph API calls are free — no per-call charges.
pricing.paid_tiers: Entra ID P1 (~$6/user/month) adds conditional access, group-based access, self-service password reset. Entra ID P2 (~$9/user/month) adds Identity Protection, Privileged Identity Management (PIM), and access reviews.
pricing.notes: API calls themselves are not metered or billed. Costs are based on per-user licensing tiers for premium features. Many identity automation use cases only require Free or P1.

performance.rate_limits: Microsoft Graph enforces service-level throttling. Default: 10,000 requests per 10 minutes per tenant. Some resources have per-resource limits. Responses include Retry-After header when throttled. Batch requests support up to 20 requests in a single HTTP call.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: Microsoft Graph returns well-structured JSON error responses with error.code and error.message fields. Common errors like Authorization_RequestDenied and Request_ResourceNotFound are well-documented. Throttling errors include Retry-After headers. Error granularity is good but some permission-denied errors are ambiguous about which scope is missing.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: GET operations are naturally idempotent. PATCH updates are idempotent for the same payload. POST creates (e.g., creating users) are not idempotent — duplicate calls create duplicate resources. Use GET-then-PATCH patterns for upsert behavior.
known_agent_gotchas: Application permissions (used in agent/daemon flows) require admin consent — agents cannot self-authorize; a tenant administrator must grant permissions via the Azure portal or consent endpoint
The Microsoft Graph API and the legacy Azure AD Graph API (graph.windows.net) are different endpoints; Azure AD Graph is deprecated — always use Microsoft Graph (graph.microsoft.com)
Delta query tokens expire after a period of inactivity — agents doing incremental sync must handle deltaLink expiration and fall back to full sync
Service principal creation requires two steps: create the application registration, then create the service principal from it — they are distinct objects with separate IDs
Throttling is per-tenant and can affect all apps in the tenant simultaneously — a noisy agent can degrade Graph API access for all other applications in the same tenant

security_score_components.security_notes: Industry-leading security posture. Certificate credentials are strongly preferred over client secrets. Managed identity eliminates all credential handling for Azure-hosted agents. Conditional access policies, PIM for just-in-time privilege, and continuous access evaluation (CAE) make this one of the most hardened identity APIs available. Entra ID supports token binding and device compliance signals.
